description_long: This object is a medieval-style wooden ballista mounted on a wheeled carriage, designed for launching large arrows or bolts. The main body is constructed from thick wooden beams, reinforced with metal brackets and bolts, and features a prominent crossbow mechanism with two large arms and taut ropes for tension. Three large, metal-tipped bolts are loaded in parallel atop the frame, ready for firing. The carriage is supported by four solid wheels, each with a metallic hub, allowing for mobility across flat surfaces. Protective side panels, likely made of wood with metal reinforcements, shield the mechanism and provide some defense for operators. The overall design is robust, functional, and clearly intended for tactical combat use in urban or battlefield scenarios.
description: A robust, wheeled wooden ballista with metal reinforcements and three large, metal-tipped bolts ready to fire. It features a crossbow mechanism, protective side panels, and four sturdy wheels for mobility.
description_view_0: Side view showing the wooden frame, three loaded bolts, and the crossbow arms with taut ropes, as well as the wheels and side panel details.
description_view_1: Front view highlighting the crossbow arms, the tips of the three bolts, and the symmetrical protective side panels.
description_view_2: Opposite side view displaying the wooden structure, loaded bolts, and the mechanical details of the crossbow and wheels.
description_view_3: Rear view focusing on the back of the frame, the arrangement of the wheels, and the tensioning ropes of the crossbow.
attribute_weapon_mounts: Three visible bolt launch rails mounted on the top frame, designed for launching large arrows or bolts.
attribute_armor_plating: Protective side panels made of wood with visible metal reinforcements, covering the sides of the main mechanism.
attribute_camouflage_pattern: None visible; the object is finished in natural wood and metal tones.
attribute_tire_type: Not applicable; the vehicle uses four solid wooden wheels with metal hubs.
